questions.question: Explain the diamond problem in inheritance with a suitable example. (3 marks)
expectedPoints: Definition: Ambiguity arising when a class inherits from two classes that share a common base.
Result: The most derived class contains multiple copies of the base class members.
Consequence: Leads to compilation errors due to ambiguous access and inefficient memory usage.

questions.question: Describe the concept of virtual base classes in C++. How do they solve the diamond problem? Also, explain the crucial rule for initializing a virtual base class. (7 marks)
expectedPoints: Concept: Virtual inheritance ensures only one instance of a base class exists in a multiple inheritance hierarchy.
Solution: The `virtual` keyword applied during inheritance (e.g., `class B : virtual public A`) makes the base class 'virtual'.
Effect: All paths to the virtual base class converge to a single, shared sub-object within the most derived class.
Eliminates Ambiguity: Direct member access becomes unambiguous because there is only one copy.
Saves Memory: Duplication of base class members is avoided.
Initialization Rule: The constructor of the MOST DERIVED class is responsible for directly initializing the virtual base class.
Syntax: This is done by explicitly calling the virtual base class's constructor in the member initialization list of the most derived class's constructor.
